findings: The headline should avoid braggadocio and instead focus on the value proposition for creators and businesses.
Use the primary color #FE2C55 wisely to highlight key phrases without overwhelming the message.
Ensure the tone remains confident yet humble, avoiding any overstatement that could alienate the target audience.

events.prompt: Build a full, self-contained marketing HTML page that incorporates the copy, layout, and imagery provided. Ensure the page follows the brand's color scheme (#FE2C55 primary, #25F4EE accent, #000000 bg

events.doing: building full HTML page with Codex (gpt-5.4)

findings: The hero leaves a large empty black void on the right, so the opening section feels unfinished instead of intentional; the page starts with type but no visual payoff or native TikTok energy.
The tiny cyan and pink corner marks in the hero read like stray punctuation rather than a deliberate brand device; they feel detached from the headline and weaken polish.
The step cards in the 'How it works' section are too narrow and tall, creating cramped line breaks that make the copy feel dense and awkward to scan.
The third step card appears pushed off the right edge in the rendered view, which makes the layout feel unstable and unfinished.
The page relies heavily on alternating black/white slabs and text cards, but it never lands a standout visual moment; the tone is competent, not playful or creatively confident enough for TikTok.
Several lines of copy are long and visually similar in weight, especially under the hero and problem sections, so hierarchy softens after the headline instead of snapping into a clear read path.

events.prompt: Audit the page against the brand rules: ensure it's bold, provocative, creative, playful, direct, casual, confident. Check for adherence to DO and DON'T guidelines, particularly avoiding business jarg

events.doing: auditing the page against brand rules

findings: The hero headline uses two different accent colors at once: the cyan plus mark and the pink word/chevron compete inside the same headline, which weakens the focal point instead of making one idea land hard.
The page is overwhelmingly text-only, especially in the hero and proof areas, so it reads more like a generic SaaS explainer than something native to TikTok’s creative, playful energy.
The pink 'How it works' band is visually blunt: one huge block of color plus three identical black cards creates a heavy slab with little rhythm, surprise, or motion.
Several lines feel like draft copy rather than finished brand writing, especially 'whatever graph you want going up' and 'That’s the whole thing,' which makes the page sound looser than polished.
The secondary hierarchy is inconsistent: body copy sizes and weights feel too similar across sections, so supporting text does not step down enough from the big headlines.
The 'Proof' section does not visually prove anything yet; it relies on generic statements instead of showing sharper, more marketer-relevant evidence cues or result framing.
The repeated black rounded cards across multiple sections become formulaic, making the layout feel system-generated rather than art-directed.
The outlined hero secondary CTA is noticeably weaker than the filled primary button and gets visually lost against the dense headline block.
The hero is visually lopsided: all the weight sits in the upper-left while the rest of the black field is mostly empty, so the opening feels unfinished instead of intentionally bold.
The tiny cyan plus and pink chevron in the hero read like stray marks rather than a deliberate brand device; they are too small to earn their space and feel disconnected from the headline.
The three white step cards in the black 'How it works' section are too narrow and too tall, forcing awkward line breaks that hurt readability and make the section feel cramped.

The 'How it works' section has weak hierarchy balance: the left headline is oversized while the instructional cards on the right feel compressed, so the eye lands on the slogan more than the actual process.
The page relies almost entirely on type blocks and plain cards, which makes the composition feel static and generic instead of playful, creator-native, and distinctly TikTok.

events.prompt: Tune the type scale and font usage to match the brand's specifications, ensuring readability and consistency. Use the provided font stack for headings and body text.

events.doing: tuning the type scale to the brand font

findings: UPGRADE: Turn the hero into a true signature `.ds-hero` art-directed moment: pair a shorter, harder headline with a bold creator-native media collage built from `.ds-figure` crops and one `.ds-bubble-cluster`, using asymmetry, rotation, and a single pink emphasis word so the page opens like an actual TikTok campaign instead of a clean landing-page template. That one move would give the whole experience a memorable visual idea, set a stronger rhythm for the sections below, and make the brand feel confidently playful rather than merely on-brand.
The hero headline uses two competing emphasis colors at once: the cyan plus mark and the pink 'Game.'/chevron all shout at the same level, so the focal point is split instead of sharp.
The page is almost entirely a stack of full-width slabs plus identical rounded black cards, which makes the layout feel repetitive and system-generated instead of playful and distinctly TikTok.

The 'Proof' section does not look like proof: '60s', '1 clear CTA', and 'More customers' read like generic placeholders rather than persuasive evidence, so the visual hierarchy promises results without showing them.
The secondary text in the hero and lower sections is visually timid against the oversized headlines, creating a drop-off in energy and making the page feel copy-heavy after the first screen.
The final CTA presents two filled buttons with near-equal visual weight, so the page ends without a single obvious action path.

events.doing: applying this turn's findings + recalled prior-turn traces
revising the page from recalled traces

findings: The hero is visually lopsided: the headline block sits on the left while a large expanse of empty black space dominates the right, making the opening feel unfinished instead of intentional.
The small cyan plus and pink chevron in the hero read like stray placeholders rather than integrated brand devices; they float without anchoring the composition.
The white 'problem' section uses three generic black cards that feel like standard SaaS feature boxes, not distinctive TikTok brand shapes or playful editorial composition.
The 'How it works' step cards are too tall and narrow, forcing awkward line breaks and making the reading rhythm feel cramped and mechanical.
The section labeled 'Proof' does not visually show proof; it presents abstract statements instead of evidence-like numbers, outcomes, or credibility cues marketers can trust at a glance.
The pink proof band is overpowering but under-structured, with a very large headline and long line lengths that flatten hierarchy instead of creating a crisp stat-driven moment.

UPGRADE: Rebuild the opening `.ds-hero` as a true signature TikTok moment: pair the existing headline with a dominant `.ds-figure` showing a vertical, native-feeling creator/video frame, overlap it with one bold `.ds-bubble-cluster`, and tighten the copy to a sharper line like 'Make 60 seconds sell.' This single move would give the page a real visual idea instead of a clean type layout, bringing in motion, personality, and platform-native confidence while keeping the stark black/white/razz palette intact.
The hero composition collapses into a big dead black void on the right; the page opens with copy only, so the top section feels unfinished instead of intentionally art-directed.
The tiny cyan and pink accent marks in the hero feel random and disconnected from the layout, reading like leftover decoration rather than a confident brand device.
The 'How it works' step cards are too tall and too narrow, forcing awkward line breaks and making the scan feel cramped instead of fast and punchy.
Hierarchy inside the hero is muddy because the bold subhead and the body copy are too close in scale and weight, so the eye does not get a crisp second step after the H1.
The overall page relies heavily on black/white text blocks and repeated rounded cards, but it does not show enough visible creator energy or platform-native texture for a TikTok brand page.

events.flaw: The page is almost entirely a stack of full-width slabs plus identical rounded black cards, which makes the layout feel repetitive and system-generated instead of playful and distinctly TikTok.

events.flaw: The 'Proof' section does not look like proof: '60s', '1 clear CTA', and 'More customers' read like generic placeholders rather than persuasive evidence, so the visual hierarchy promises results without showing them.

events.flaw: The secondary text in the hero and lower sections is visually timid against the oversized headlines, creating a drop-off in energy and making the page feel copy-heavy after the first screen.
The final CTA presents two filled buttons with near-equal visual weight, so the page ends without a single obvious action path.

events.summary: Do not reintroduce this already-fixed issue: The hero headline uses two competing emphasis colors at once: the cyan plus mark and the pink 'Game.'/chevron all shout at the same level, so the focal point is split instead of sharp. (brand rule: no more than one emphasis color per headline)

Do not reintroduce this already-fixed issue: The page is almost entirely a stack of full-width slabs plus identical rounded black cards, which makes the layout feel repetitive and system-generated instead of playful and distinctly TikTok. (brand rule: Forget to have some fun along the way)

(brand rule: Forget you're talking to marketers who need results)

Do not reintroduce this already-fixed issue: The secondary text in the hero and lower sections is visually timid against the oversized headlines, creating a drop-off in energy and making the page feel copy-heavy after the first screen. (brand rule: Try too hard)

Do not reintroduce this already-fixed issue: The final CTA presents two filled buttons with near-equal visual weight, so the page ends without a single obvious action path. (brand rule: Forget you're talking to marketers who need results)

events.reasoning: The page has a strong hero, high contrast, and real persuasive copy, with clear headline-to-CTA hierarchy and generally confident type scale. Craft drops in the body: the middle card row feels visually awkward with the oversized curved black shape clipping content, spacing gets heavy and uneven between sections, and the lower section is cut off in a way that weakens rhythm. Brand fit is not fully compliant because a large white/neutral background section is prominently used despite the stated 'no grey/neutral backgrounds' rule, and the cyan accent is essentially absent, so the rendered page does not feel fully unmistakable to this brand. quality 76, brandAdherence 44 → score 63 (0.6·q + 0.4·b, capped at 69 for brand miss).

The page has strong contrast, a clear hero headline, and real benefit-led copy, but craft slips in the oversized hero illustration, the large dead black space around it, and slightly uneven section-to-section rhythm. Brand fit is recognizable through the black/white/pink/cyan palette and playful pills, but the hero headline visibly uses two emphasis colors at once (cyan mark plus pink word/bracket), which breaks the hard rule of no more than one emphasis color per headline and forces the score below 50. quality 78, brandAdherence 43 → score 64 (0.6·q + 0.4·b, capped at 69 for brand miss).

events.doing: recalling prior in-run traces from memory

findings: The 'Proof' headline reads as broken copy: 'Small videos can make a very big. difference.' The period after 'big' looks like an error, not a deliberate brand move.
The right-hand 'What usually goes wrong' card squeezes three text columns so tightly that line breaks feel accidental and choppy, which hurts scanability and makes the section feel under-designed.

The page is almost entirely text blocks and flat cards, so it misses the playful, creator-native energy TikTok should have; visually it feels more like a stern landing page than a living platform brand.
The 'How it works' band is bold in color but not in composition: three identical black cards on a solid pink field flatten the hierarchy and make the section feel repetitive instead of dynamic.
The small cyan and pink brand marks near the hero headline read too tiny to matter at page scale, so they feel decorative rather than intentional and do not carry enough visual personality.
The 'See the payoff' button at the bottom of the pink section feels visually stranded, with weak connection to the cards above and too little presence for a key transition CTA.

events.prompt: Audit the page against the brand rules, focusing on adherence to color usage, font selection, tone, and avoiding business jargon. List specific findings and ensure all elements are on-brand.

events.doing: auditing the page against brand rules

findings: The 'Proof' headline reads as a broken sentence: 'Small videos can make a very big. difference.' It looks like placeholder or unfinished copy and undercuts trust immediately.
The cyan plus and pink chevron in the hero feel tiny and disconnected from the headline, more like stray punctuation than confident brand devices.
The white 'problem' section is so restrained and text-heavy that it loses the playful, creator-native energy the brand needs; it starts to feel like a generic B2B landing page.

The action hierarchy is uneven: the secondary outline CTA in the hero is visually timid next to the primary button, while the lone white pill in the pink section feels detached from the content block above it.
The three narrow text columns inside 'What usually goes wrong' are hard to scan at a glance because the line breaks are awkward and the cards don't create distinct visual entry points.
